Chapter 1 ✓
02 · Actions & Composition 
action revealBlock(selector) [
  selectElement(selector)
  addClass("in")
  addClass("typing")
]
action fadeIn(selector, duration: number) [
  selectElement(selector)
  animate({ opacity: 1 }, duration)
]
at 0s..5s fadeIn("#hero", 400)   // your action
at 0s..5s addClass("on")         // built-in op
action narrate(template) [
  selectElement("#narration")
  setElementContent(template)
]
endable action highlight(selector) [
  selectElement(selector)
  addClass("spot")
] [
  selectElement(selector)   // re-select: nothing carries over
  removeClass("spot")
]
library presentation
action revealBlock(selector) [ … ]       // public
private action _helper() [ … ]      // library-only

import { revealBlock, narrate } from "./presentation.eligian"

03 · Logic & Data 
const theme = "dark"      // → $globaldata.theme

action greet(name) [
  const greeting = "hi"    // → @greeting
  setElementContent(greeting)
]
$operationdata.count + 1
$operationdata.count >= 3 && @enabled
!$globaldata.muted
if (count > 0) {
  addClass("active")
} else {
  removeClass("active")
}
setVariable("points", ["#c3-b1", "#c3-b2", "#c3-b3"])
for (p in $scope.variables.points) {
  selectElement(@@currentItem)
  addClass("in")
}
for (n in @numbers) {
  if (@@currentItem == 0) { continue }
  if (@@currentItem > 9) { break }
}
$operationdata.x   // this block's data
$globaldata.x      // program-wide
$scope.x           // loop / branch scope
$globaldata$scope.variables
2 + 1 → 33 >= 3 → true
badge
  • if / else branches
  • for iterates a collection
  • break / continue steer it

Chapter 4 ✓
05 · Engine Reach 
broadcastEvent([args], "name")            // 1. the eventbus
on event "name" action H(p) [ … ]    // 2. react to it
addController("…Controller", …)        // 3. attach behaviour
broadcastEvent(["#c5-fx-panel"], "tour-ping")        // broadcast: all listeners
broadcastEvent(["Hub", 0], "request-timeline-uri")  // request: engine answers
on event "tour-ping" action onTourPing(target) [
  selectElement(target)        // target ← eventArgs[0]
  addClass("in")
]
selectElement("#c5-fx-ping")
addController("DOMEventListenerController",
  "click", ["broadcastPing"])   // event, then actions to run
locales "./locales/en.json"

selectElement("#c5-fx-label")
addController("LabelController", "tour.greeting")
broadcastEvent(["Hub",0], "request-timeline-uri")  // ← every card you clicked
addController("DOMEventListenerController", …)   // ← what navigate desugars to
addController("LabelController", …)             // ← the localized narration
eventbuson eventcontrollers
handler answered — panel revealed
en → Hellonl → Hallo

Tour ✓ — the whole language

actionOperationData.template: An Eligian presentation is one or more timelines. Here's the one you're watching.
its name — also the uri the hub uses to jump here
What advances the timeline? Its provider.
raf = animation clock; video / audio / custom also available
Inside a timeline, 'at' schedules work over a time range.
a time range: start .. end
Times take units. No unit means milliseconds.
Ranges can compute — and chain off the previous event.
relative time — anchored to the previous event's end
That whole sequence? One timeline. Click ← Hub to explore more.
An action is a named list of operations. Define once, call anywhere.
declares a reusable action; its body runs top-to-bottom
Actions take parameters. Annotate types when you want the compiler's help.
optional type — string · number · boolean · object · array
Calling your action looks exactly like calling a built-in operation.
one syntax; resolved action-first, then built-in op
A parameter that feeds a built-in op must be named after that op's input.
selectElement→selector · addClass→className · setElementContent→template
An endable action has two blocks: set up, then tear down — independently.
start & end run fresh; re-select in each — only the DOM persists
Group actions into a library, import what you need. Click ← Hub for more.
const names a value. In an action it's a scope variable; at top level it's global.
action scope → @name · program scope → $globaldata
Expressions combine values: arithmetic, comparison, logic.
if / else runs operations conditionally — compiles to when / otherwise / endWhen.
each branch is its own operation block
for iterates a collection — and this list is being revealed by one right now.
@@currentItem is the current item; compiles to forEach / endForEach
break exits the loop; continue skips to the next item — only inside a for.
continue → continueForEach · break → breakForEach
Three places data lives: $operationdata, $globaldata, $scope. Click ← Hub for more.
Scheduling a chain of events by hand means recomputing every time you tweak one.
change one length and every later time is re-done by hand
'sequence' lays events back-to-back automatically — each runs 'for' a duration.
start = previous end → 0s..5s · 5s..15s · 15s..18s
Because starts are computed, changing one duration shifts everything after it.
shorten 'main' and 'outro' slides left — no re-numbering
'stagger' fans one effect across a collection — each item starts a little after the last.
200ms delay between starts · the item feeds the action's first arg
delay is the gap between starts; duration is how long each lasts — they overlap on purpose.
inline start/end op blocks instead of 'with <action>'
'sequence' for distinct back-to-back steps; 'stagger' for one effect across many items.
Everything so far fired on the timeline clock. But a presentation also has to react — that's engine reach.
three doors past the clock: the eventbus, on event, controllers
broadcastEvent puts a message on the engine's bus. Broadcast reaches every listener; request asks for the one that answers.
request-timeline-uri is the request the navigate sugar emits
An on event action runs when its event fires, not at a time. Click the button — it broadcasts, and this handler answers.
params map to the event's args by position: target is eventArgs[0]
A controller attaches lasting behaviour to an element. The button's click is wired by a DOMEventListenerController — one of eight.
selectElement + addController; this is what navigate desugars to
Locales make text translatable. A LabelController binds a translation key to an element and re-renders on language-change.
translationKey resolved via the rosetta locale system
Eventbus, on event, controllers, locales — the navigation and narration you've used were built from exactly these. Click ← Hub for more.
Eligian isn't just shorter Eligius JSON — it's a typed, tooled way to write it. Everything you've watched was authored with these tools.
concise on the left, type-safe everywhere, live in the preview
Preview Timeline compiles your .eligian on the fly, runs it in an embedded engine, and reloads on every save. This tour is what you'd see there.
compiles + runs in a webview engine; a FileWatcher reloads on save
Every class and selector is checked against your imported CSS. Typo one and you get a squiggle, a suggestion, and a one-click fix.
CSS class 'buton' is not defined. Did you mean: button?
Type and the editor knows what fits: operations with their parameters, the eight controllers, label IDs, CSS classes, even an on event skeleton.
operations show their signature and docs; controllers list their params
Document an action with @param and the editor hovers those docs at every call — and flags wrong argument counts, unknown ops, bad nav or event names.
@param docs on hover; semantic checks catch the rest
Tweak your CSS and it hot-reloads into the running preview. Edit translations in a dedicated editor. Concise, type-safe, live — that's Eligian. Click ← Hub.
